title: Environmental DNA
text: Content

title: The molecular foundation of environmental DNA
text:
Life as an information system
Every environmental DNA (eDNA) study begins with a simple question: how can a few microscopic fragments of DNA in a sample reveal which organisms live in an ecosystem? The answer lies in understanding DNA itself.
Environmental DNA analysis is fundamentally an application of molecular biology. Before learning how DNA is collected, amplified, sequenced, and analysed, it is essential to understand what DNA is, how it is organised within cells, and why its sequence can be used to identify organisms.
Every living organism, from bacteria to whales, must perform the same fundamental tasks. It must obtain energy, maintain its internal environment, respond to external conditions, reproduce, and pass hereditary information to the next generation. These processes require instructions which are stored in DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid).
Rather than viewing DNA simply as a chemical molecule, it is more helpful to think of it as the biological information storage system of life. Just as a computer stores information as binary code, living organisms store biological information as sequences of four nucleotide bases:
The order of these bases forms a molecular language. Every cell interprets this language to manufacture proteins, regulate metabolism, and coordinate development.
Importantly, different species possess different DNA sequences. Although much of the genome is shared because all organisms perform similar cellular functions, enough sequence variation exists to distinguish one species from another.
This principle forms the foundation of environmental DNA analysis.
crumbs: 2  The molecular foundation of environmental DNA

title: The molecular foundation of environmental DNA
text:
Adenine (A)
Thymine (T)
Cytosine (C)
Guanine (G)







Nucleotide bases
crumbs: 2  The molecular foundation of environmental DNA

title: The molecular foundation of environmental DNA
section: The cell: the basic unit of life
text:
The cell: the basic unit of life
Cells are the smallest structural and functional units capable of independent life. Each cell contains specialised components known as organelles, each performing a particular function.
Examples include:

Nucleus: stores most genetic information
Mitochondria: produce ATP through cellular respiration
Ribosomes: synthesise proteins
Endoplasmic reticulum and Golgi apparatus: process and transport proteins;
Lysosomes and peroxisomes: recycle cellular materials.

Although cells differ greatly among organisms, almost every eukaryotic cell follows the same fundamental organisational plan.
The genetic information that controls cellular function is largely contained within the nucleus.
crumbs: 2  The molecular foundation of environmental DNA

title: The molecular foundation of environmental DNA
crumbs: 2  The molecular foundation of environmental DNA

title: The molecular foundation of environmental DNA
text:
Chromosomes: organising the genome
Human DNA would measure approximately two metres if stretched into a straight line. Yet this enormous molecule must fit inside a nucleus only a few micrometres in diameter. This remarkable packaging is achieved through chromosomes.
DNA wraps around histone proteins to form chromatin. The chromatin fibre is folded repeatedly until individual chromosomes are produced. Chromosomes therefore serve two important purposes:

First, they compact DNA so it fits inside the nucleus.
Second, they organise genetic information so that DNA can be accurately copied and inherited during cell division.

Humans possess 46 chromosomes arranged as 23 pairs. Other species possess different chromosome numbers, but chromosome number itself does not indicate biological complexity.
crumbs: 2  The molecular foundation of environmental DNA

title: The molecular foundation of environmental DNA
text:
Genes: functional units within DNA
Genes are specific regions of DNA that contain information required to produce functional molecules. Most genes encode proteins, although some produce functional RNA molecules that never become proteins. A gene therefore represents a functional unit rather than an independent molecule.
Genes occupy particular positions, known as loci, along chromosomes.
Between genes lie extensive non-coding regions that regulate when genes are switched on or off, influence chromosome organisation, and contribute to genome stability.
Modern molecular biology recognises that these non-coding regions are often just as important as protein-coding genes.
crumbs: 2  The molecular foundation of environmental DNA

title: The molecular foundation of environmental DNA
section: From DNA sequence to biological function
text:
From DNA sequence to biological function
DNA does not directly perform most cellular functions.
Instead, genetic information flows through a sequence of molecular events known as the central dogma of molecular biology: DNA → RNA → Protein
DNA stores information.
RNA copies and transports that information.
Proteins perform most biological functions. Proteins act as enzymes, structural molecules, receptors, transport proteins and signalling molecules.
Ultimately, every observable biological characteristic arises because DNA sequences influence which proteins are produced and when they are expressed.
crumbs: 2  The molecular foundation of environmental DNA

title: The molecular foundation of environmental DNA
text:
Why DNA differs between species
All vertebrates require proteins for respiration, muscle contraction and metabolism. Consequently, many genes are highly conserved through evolution. However, mutations accumulate gradually over millions of years. Some mutations alter DNA sequences without affecting survival. These neutral mutations accumulate independently within different evolutionary lineages. As species diverge, their DNA sequences gradually become increasingly distinct. Environmental DNA analysis exploits these accumulated differences.
Rather than examining an entire genome, researchers analyse short regions that are:

sufficiently conserved to allow universal amplification;
sufficiently variable to distinguish species.

These regions are known as genetic markers and form the foundation of DNA barcoding and metabarcoding.
crumbs: 2  The molecular foundation of environmental DNA

title: The molecular foundation of environmental DNA
section: Why we do not sequence entire genomes
text:
Why we do not sequence entire genomes
At first glance, sequencing the complete genome of an organism might appear ideal. In environmental samples, however, this is neither practical nor necessary. Water, soil and sediment contain DNA from thousands of organisms simultaneously. Most environmental DNA is fragmented, damaged and present in extremely small quantities. Instead of sequencing billions of base pairs, researchers target short diagnostic regions that provide enough variation for species identification. This approach dramatically reduces sequencing costs while increasing detection sensitivity.
crumbs: 2  The molecular foundation of environmental DNA

title: The molecular foundation of environmental DNA
text:
Connecting the concepts
The concepts introduced in this chapter form a biological hierarchy.
Living organisms are composed of cells that contain a nucleus, within which chromosomes organise the genome. Chromosomes consist of DNA molecules that contain genes which is the functional units of genetic information. Through gene expression, this information is used to produce RNA and proteins, which regulate cellular processes and ultimately determine the structure, function, and biological characteristics of an organism.
Environmental DNA analysis begins when fragments of this hierarchy enter the environment after cells are shed, damaged or die.
crumbs: 2  The molecular foundation of environmental DNA

title: The molecular foundation of environmental DNA
text:
Key concepts

DNA is the universal hereditary material of almost all living organisms.
The genome represents all genetic information within an organism.
Chromosomes package and organise DNA.
Genes are functional regions of DNA.
DNA sequence differences allow species to be distinguished.
Environmental DNA analysis identifies organisms by analysing short diagnostic DNA sequences rather than whole genomes.






The relationship between genes, DNA and chromosomes
crumbs: 2  The molecular foundation of environmental DNA

title: From living organisms to environmental DNA
text:
What is environmental DNA?
In the previous chapter, we learned that DNA stores the genetic information required to build and maintain every living organism. However, environmental DNA analysis does not study DNA inside living cells. Instead, it relies on fragments of genetic material that have been released into the environment. This immediately raises an important question.
How does DNA leave an organism and become environmental DNA?
Answering this question is essential because every stage of eDNA analysis depends on understanding where environmental DNA originates, how it changes after release, and why only a small fraction survives long enough to be detected.
Environmental DNA (eDNA) is genetic material recovered directly from environmental samples such as water, soil, sediment, snow, ice or air without first isolating the target organisms. Importantly, eDNA is not chemically different from DNA inside a living cell. The difference lies entirely in its location.
Inside an organism, DNA is protected by cellular membranes, organelles, proteins and repair systems. Once released into the environment, these protective mechanisms disappear, and DNA becomes exposed to physical, chemical and biological degradation.
Consequently, environmental DNA should be viewed as ordinary DNA existing under extraordinary conditions.
crumbs: 3  From living organisms to environmental DNA

title: From living organisms to environmental DNA
text:
Sources of environmental DNA
Every organism continuously releases genetic material into its surroundings. This release occurs throughout life and continues after death. Major sources include shed skin cells, mucus, faeces, urine, saliva, blood, scales, feathers and hair, reproductive cells, decomposing tissues and decaying carcasses. Aquatic organisms constantly release cells into surrounding water, while terrestrial organisms contribute DNA to soil and to the atmosphere. Environmental DNA therefore represents a mixture of genetic material originating from many individuals and species simultaneously.
crumbs: 3  From living organisms to environmental DNA

title: From living organisms to environmental DNA
text:
Intracellular and extracellular DNA
Once released, environmental DNA exists in two principal forms.

Intracellular DNA
Some DNA remains enclosed within intact or partially intact cells, mitochondria or other cellular structures. These membranes provide temporary protection from degradation, increasing the likelihood that DNA survives long enough for detection.


Extracellular DNA
Other DNA exists freely within the environment.
Extracellular DNA may:

dissolve in water
adsorb onto clay minerals
bind to organic particles
become incorporated into sediments
attach to biofilms

Extracellular DNA is generally more vulnerable to enzymatic degradation but may persist surprisingly long when protected by mineral surfaces.
crumbs: 3  From living organisms to environmental DNA

title: From living organisms to environmental DNA
text:
Environmental transport
DNA rarely remains where it was released. Water currents transport DNA downstream. Wind redistributes airborne particles. Sediments trap DNA and may later release it. Animals themselves may transport DNA on their bodies or within their digestive systems. Consequently, detecting DNA does not necessarily indicate that the organism was present at the precise sampling location. Instead, environmental DNA reflects a combination of biological activity, environmental transport and degradation. Understanding these processes is essential when interpreting survey results.
crumbs: 3  From living organisms to environmental DNA

title: From living organisms to environmental DNA
text:
DNA degradation
Once outside living cells, DNA immediately begins to degrade. Several processes contribute simultaneously.

Enzymatic degradation
Microorganisms release nucleases that digest DNA into progressively smaller fragments.


Ultraviolet radiation
Sunlight damages DNA by producing lesions such as thymine dimers, reducing successful amplification.


Temperature
Higher temperatures accelerate both chemical reactions and microbial activity, increasing degradation rates.


pH
Highly acidic or alkaline environments promote hydrolysis of chemical bonds within DNA.


Mechanical processes
Water movement, sediment abrasion and repeated freeze–thaw cycles physically fragment DNA molecules.
These mechanisms rarely operate independently. Instead, environmental DNA usually experiences several degradation processes simultaneously.
crumbs: 3  From living organisms to environmental DNA

title: From living organisms to environmental DNA
text:
Why environmental DNA is fragmented
Unlike genomic DNA extracted directly from fresh tissue, environmental DNA is rarely intact. Instead, it consists primarily of short fragments. In aquatic environments, fragments shorter than approximately 200 base pairs are particularly common. This fragmentation has profound consequences for molecular analysis.
Long DNA targets are unlikely to remain intact. Short targets survive much more frequently. This simple observation explains why most environmental DNA studies amplify fragments between approximately 80 and 150 base pairs.
Marker choice is therefore driven not only by evolutionary biology but also by molecular degradation.

Persistence
Environmental DNA does not disappear immediately after an organism leaves an area. Persistence depends on environmental conditions. In flowing rivers, detectable DNA may remain for only hours or days. In lakes, persistence may extend to several weeks. Within sediments, protected DNA may survive for months or even years. Persistence increases the probability of detection but also introduces uncertainty. DNA may indicate recent rather than current presence. Consequently, eDNA surveys must always be interpreted within their environmental context.
crumbs: 3  From living organisms to environmental DNA

title: From living organisms to environmental DNA
text:
The journey of environmental DNA
Living organisms shed cells and tissues, releasing DNA into the environment, where it is transported by water, wind, or sediments before undergoing fragmentation and degradation. This environmental DNA is then sampled, extracted, amplified by PCR, sequenced, and used for species identification.
crumbs: 3  From living organisms to environmental DNA

title: From living organisms to environmental DNA
text:
Key concepts

Environmental DNA is ordinary DNA found outside living organisms.
DNA originates from many biological sources.
Environmental DNA exists in both intracellular and extracellular forms.
DNA is transported through the environment before sampling.
Physical, chemical and biological processes fragment DNA continuously.
Environmental degradation strongly influences marker selection and laboratory methods.
crumbs: 3  From living organisms to environmental DNA

title: DNA structure and function
text:
DNA as a polymer
Environmental DNA (eDNA) analysis depends on one remarkable property of DNA: although it begins to degrade once released into the environment, fragments often remain sufficiently intact to be detected, amplified and sequenced. This persistence is possible because DNA is a chemically stable molecule capable of storing biological information over long periods of time.
To understand why eDNA methods work, it is necessary to understand how DNA is built, how it stores genetic information, and how its molecular structure influences stability, replication and detection. These same structural features determine where PCR primers bind, why short DNA fragments can be successfully amplified, and how DNA sequences can be used to distinguish one species from another.
DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) is a long biological polymer composed of repeating units called nucleotides. Each nucleotide contributes one “letter” to the DNA sequence, and the precise order of these nucleotides stores the hereditary information of an organism.
Unlike proteins, which are built from twenty different amino acids, DNA is constructed from only four types of nucleotides. Despite this apparent simplicity, the enormous number of possible nucleotide combinations enables DNA to encode the genetic information required for the development, function and reproduction of every known organism.

title: DNA structure and function
text:
The structure of a nucleotide
Each nucleotide consists of three components:

a five-carbon sugar (deoxyribose);
a phosphate group;
a nitrogen-containing base.

The sugar and phosphate form the structural framework of DNA, while the nitrogenous bases carry the genetic information.
The four bases found in DNA are:

Adenine (A)
Thymine (T)
Cytosine (C)
Guanine (G)

The order, or sequence, of these bases stores the genetic information of an organism.

title: DNA structure and function
text:
Building a DNA strand
Individual nucleotides are joined together by phosphodiester bonds. These covalent bonds link the phosphate group attached to the 5′ carbon of one nucleotide to the hydroxyl group attached to the 3′ carbon of the next nucleotide. As nucleotides are added, they form a continuous sugar–phosphate backbone. Importantly, the backbone is chemically uniform. The only part of the molecule that varies is the sequence of nitrogenous bases, which projects inward from the backbone. This distinction explains why DNA can maintain a stable overall structure while simultaneously storing enormous amounts of genetic information.

title: DNA structure and function
text:
The double helix
DNA usually exists as two complementary strands twisted around one another to form the familiar double helix. The sugar–phosphate backbones lie on the outside of the molecule, while the nitrogenous bases project inward. Bases pair in a highly specific manner:

Adenine pairs with thymine through two hydrogen bonds.
Guanine pairs with cytosine through three hydrogen bonds.

These complementary base pairs hold the two strands together while maintaining the constant diameter of the double helix. Because each strand determines the sequence of its partner, either strand can serve as a template for DNA replication.

title: DNA structure and function
text:
Antiparallel orientation
The two DNA strands do not run in the same direction. Instead, they are antiparallel. One strand runs from the 5′ end to the 3′ end, while the complementary strand runs from 3′ to 5′. This orientation is determined by the numbering of carbon atoms within the deoxyribose sugar. Although this arrangement may appear to be a structural detail, it has major biological consequences. DNA polymerases can synthesise DNA only by adding nucleotides to the free 3′ hydroxyl group of a growing strand. Consequently, every process involving DNA replication, repair and PCR depends on strand directionality.

title: DNA structure and function
section: DNA sequence: the language of biology
text:
DNA sequence: the language of biology
The order of nucleotides within DNA forms the biological equivalent of a written language. Just as changing a letter alters the meaning of a word, changing a nucleotide can alter genetic information.
For example:
ATGCTTAC…
and
ATGCGTAC…
differ by only a single nucleotide.
Although the difference appears small, it may distinguish one species from another or alter the function of a gene. Environmental DNA analysis exploits these sequence differences to identify organisms from tiny DNA fragments recovered from environmental samples.

title: DNA structure and function
text:
The genetic code
The sequence of nucleotides in DNA contains the instructions needed to build proteins. However, proteins are composed of amino acids rather than nucleotides, so this genetic information must first be translated into a different molecular language.
The first step is transcription, in which the DNA sequence is copied into messenger RNA (mRNA). The mRNA molecule carries the genetic instructions from the nucleus to ribosomes, where proteins are synthesised. During translation, the mRNA sequence is read according to a set of rules known as the genetic code.
The genetic code is read in groups of three nucleotides, called codons. Each codon specifies either one amino acid or a signal to begin or end protein synthesis. For example, the codon ATG (or AUG in mRNA) encodes the amino acid methionine and usually serves as the start codon, whereas TAA, TAG and TGA function as stop codons.
Because each codon contains three nucleotide positions and each position can contain one of four bases, there are 4 × 4 × 4 = 64 possible codons. Of these, 61 encode the 20 standard amino acids, while three function as stop signals. Since several codons specify the same amino acid, the genetic code is described as degenerate, or redundant.
Although the genetic code explains how DNA directs protein synthesis, most eDNA studies do not analyse proteins. Instead, they examine the DNA sequence itself. As DNA sequences accumulate mutations over evolutionary time, they become increasingly different among species, providing the sequence variation required for species identification.



Genetic code

title: DNA structure and function
text:
Conserved and variable regions
Not all DNA evolves at the same rate. Some regions perform essential biological functions and therefore change very little during evolution. These are known as conserved regions. Other regions accumulate mutations more rapidly and differ substantially among species. These are referred to as variable regions. This combination of conservation and variation makes molecular identification possible. Conserved regions provide stable binding sites for PCR primers. The variable sequence located between these primer binding sites contains the information required to distinguish species. This principle underlies nearly every eDNA metabarcoding assay.

title: DNA structure and function
section: From DNA structure to environmental DNA
text:
From DNA structure to environmental DNA
The molecular structure of DNA underpins every stage of environmental DNA analysis. After DNA is released into the environment, its chemical stability allows some fragments to survive despite continuous degradation. During PCR, the DNA strands are separated by heating, allowing primers to bind to conserved target regions. DNA polymerase then amplifies the intervening variable region, producing millions of copies for sequencing. The resulting sequences are compared with reference databases to identify the organisms that contributed the environmental DNA.

title: DNA structure and function
text:
Connecting the concepts
Nucleotides are linked together by phosphodiester bonds to form DNA strands, and two complementary strands pair to create the DNA double helix. The sequence of nucleotides stores genetic information, which is interpreted according to the genetic code to produce proteins. Over evolutionary time, mutations generate conserved and variable regions within DNA. Conserved regions provide binding sites for PCR primers, while the intervening variable regions are amplified and sequenced to enable species identification.

title: DNA structure and function
text:
Key concepts
At the end of this chapter, the most important ideas are:

DNA is composed of repeating nucleotide units.
Nucleotides are linked by phosphodiester bonds to form a sugar–phosphate backbone.
Two complementary DNA strands form the double helix.
DNA strands are antiparallel.
Genetic information is stored in the sequence of nucleotides.
The genetic code translates nucleotide triplets (codons) into amino acids during protein synthesis.
Conserved regions provide primer-binding sites, while variable regions enable species identification in eDNA analyses.

title: Mitochondrial DNA
text:
What are mitochondria?
In the previous chapter, we learned that DNA stores genetic information in the sequence of nucleotide bases and that differences in these sequences allow species to be distinguished. However, environmental samples contain only tiny amounts of highly fragmented DNA originating from many different organisms. This raises an important question:
If every cell contains thousands of genes, why do most eDNA studies analyse only a few specific regions of mitochondrial DNA?
The answer lies in the unique biological properties of mitochondria. Compared with nuclear DNA, mitochondrial DNA is present in many more copies per cell, has a compact genome, and contains genes that combine highly conserved regions with species-specific variation. These characteristics make mitochondrial DNA particularly well suited for detecting organisms from trace amounts of degraded environmental DNA.
Mitochondria are membrane-bound organelles found in almost all eukaryotic cells. Their primary role is to produce adenosine triphosphate (ATP), the universal energy currency of the cell, through cellular respiration.
Unlike most organelles, mitochondria possess their own genome. This is because they evolved from free-living bacteria that entered into a symbiotic relationship with ancestral eukaryotic cells more than 1.5 billion years ago. Although most of the original bacterial genes have been transferred to the nucleus during evolution, mitochondria have retained a small circular genome that is replicated independently of nuclear DNA.
This evolutionary history explains why mitochondria contain their own DNA and why mitochondrial genes differ from those found in the nucleus.

title: Mitochondrial DNA
text: Mitochondria

title: Mitochondrial DNA
text:
The mitochondrial genome
The mitochondrial genome is much smaller than the nuclear genome. For example, the human nuclear genome contains approximately 3.2 billion base pairs, whereas the mitochondrial genome consists of only about 16,500 base pairs. Despite its small size, mitochondrial DNA contains genes that are essential for cellular respiration, including protein-coding genes, transfer RNA (tRNA) genes and ribosomal RNA (rRNA) genes.
Unlike nuclear chromosomes, mitochondrial DNA is:

circular rather than linear
present in many copies per cell
inherited almost exclusively from the mother in most animals
not packaged into chromosomes

Although compact, the mitochondrial genome contains sufficient sequence variation to distinguish many species.

title: Mitochondrial DNA
section: Why mitochondrial DNA is ideal for eDNA
text:
Why mitochondrial DNA is ideal for eDNA
Environmental DNA studies are essentially a search for extremely rare DNA fragments. The probability of detecting DNA depends largely on how many copies of the target sequence are present in the original sample. Each eukaryotic cell contains only two copies of most nuclear genes but may contain hundreds to thousands of copies of mitochondrial DNA because every cell contains numerous mitochondria, each carrying multiple copies of its genome. This dramatically increases the probability that at least one copy of a mitochondrial gene survives environmental degradation and is recovered during sampling.
The large number of mitochondrial genomes present in each cell means that many copies of the target DNA are released into the environment. Although most fragments eventually degrade, the high initial copy number increases the likelihood that some remain intact long enough to be sampled, amplified and sequenced, thereby improving detection sensitivity.
For this reason, mitochondrial DNA is generally much easier to detect than nuclear DNA in environmental samples.

title: Mitochondrial DNA
text:
Mitochondrial genes used in eDNA
Not every mitochondrial gene is equally useful. An ideal genetic marker should satisfy two requirements:

Conserved regions that allow universal PCR primers to bind across many species.
Variable regions that differ sufficiently to distinguish species.

Several mitochondrial genes meet these criteria.

12S ribosomal RNA (12S rRNA)
The 12S rRNA gene is one of the most widely used markers for vertebrate eDNA studies. Its short variable regions are flanked by highly conserved sequences, allowing universal primers to amplify DNA from a wide range of fish, amphibians, reptiles, birds and mammals.


Cytochrome c oxidase subunit I (COI)
COI is the standard DNA barcode for animals and provides excellent species resolution. However, the full-length barcode is relatively long and often unsuitable for degraded environmental DNA unless shorter “mini-barcode” regions are used.


Cytochrome b (Cyt b)
Cytochrome b is frequently used in phylogenetic studies and species identification but is less commonly employed in high-throughput metabarcoding because it generally requires longer DNA fragments.
The choice of marker therefore depends on the organisms being studied, the condition of the DNA and the objectives of the investigation.



Mitochondrial DNA

title: Mitochondrial DNA
text:
Mitochondrial DNA is not perfect
Although mitochondrial DNA offers many advantages, it also has limitations. Because it is inherited maternally in most animals, mitochondrial DNA represents only a single genetic lineage. It therefore provides less information about population genetics than nuclear DNA. Some closely related species possess nearly identical mitochondrial sequences, making reliable discrimination difficult. In addition, the number of mitochondrial genomes varies among tissues and species, meaning that mitochondrial DNA abundance does not necessarily reflect organism abundance. Consequently, eDNA studies detect DNA, not individuals.

title: Mitochondrial DNA
section: From mitochondrial DNA to species identification
text:
From mitochondrial DNA to species identification
Living cells contain numerous mitochondria, and each mitochondrion carries multiple copies of mitochondrial DNA. When cells are shed or die, these mitochondrial genomes are released into the environment. Although many DNA molecules degrade over time, some fragments remain sufficiently intact to be recovered during sampling. In the laboratory, PCR amplifies short mitochondrial DNA markers, which are then sequenced. The resulting sequences are compared with reference databases to identify the species from which the environmental DNA originated.

title: Mitochondrial DNA
text:
Comparing nuclear and mitochondrial DNA








Feature
Nuclear DNA
Mitochondrial DNA




Location
Nucleus
Mitochondria


Structure
Linear chromosomes
Circular genome


Copies per cell
Two
Hundreds to thousands


Genome size
Very large
Small


Inheritance
Biparental
Usually maternal


Detection in eDNA
Lower sensitivity
Higher sensitivity


Primary use
Population genetics, genomics
Species detection and metabarcoding

title: Mitochondrial DNA
text:
Key concepts
At the end of this chapter, the most important ideas are:

Mitochondria possess their own independent genome.
Mitochondrial DNA is present in many more copies than nuclear DNA.
High copy number increases the probability of detecting degraded environmental DNA.
Mitochondrial genes contain both conserved and variable regions, making them excellent genetic markers.
Mitochondrial DNA is highly effective for species detection but has important biological limitations.

title: Genetic markers and primer design
text:
What is a genetic marker?
In the previous chapter, we learned that mitochondrial DNA is widely used in environmental DNA (eDNA) studies because its high copy number increases the probability of detecting degraded DNA fragments. However, sequencing an entire mitochondrial genome from every organism present in an environmental sample is neither practical nor necessary.
Instead, eDNA studies target short regions of DNA known as genetic markers. These markers contain sufficient sequence variation to distinguish species while remaining conserved enough to allow universal amplification using PCR.
Selecting the appropriate genetic marker is one of the most important decisions in any eDNA study. Marker choice determines which organisms can be detected, the taxonomic resolution that can be achieved, and the overall reliability of species identification.
This chapter introduces the principles of genetic marker selection and explains how PCR primers are designed to amplify the most informative regions of DNA.
A genetic marker is a specific region of DNA used to identify organisms or distinguish between species. Rather than analysing an entire genome, researchers examine relatively short DNA sequences that contain informative genetic variation.
The ideal marker should satisfy two important criteria:
These properties allow DNA from many organisms to be amplified simultaneously while retaining enough sequence variation for species identification.
crumbs: 6  Genetic markers and primer design

title: Genetic markers and primer design
text:
it should be highly conserved at its ends so that universal PCR primers can bind across many species;
it should contain a variable internal region that differs among species and provides taxonomic information.
crumbs: 6  Genetic markers and primer design

title: Genetic markers and primer design
text:
DNA barcoding and metabarcoding
Traditional DNA barcoding identifies a single organism by sequencing one standard genetic marker.
In contrast, DNA metabarcoding analyses environmental samples that contain DNA from many species simultaneously. Universal primers amplify the same genetic marker from all organisms present in the sample, and high-throughput sequencing generates millions of DNA sequences that are assigned to individual taxa using reference databases.
While DNA barcoding focuses on individual specimens, metabarcoding provides a comprehensive assessment of entire biological communities.
crumbs: 6  Genetic markers and primer design

title: Genetic markers and primer design
section: Characteristics of an ideal genetic marker
text:
Characteristics of an ideal genetic marker
No single genetic marker is suitable for every organism or research question. However, an effective marker generally possesses several important characteristics.
An ideal marker should:

be present in all target organisms
contain conserved primer-binding regions
include sufficient sequence variation to distinguish species
be short enough to amplify degraded environmental DNA
have comprehensive reference sequences available in public databases

Balancing these characteristics is often the greatest challenge in marker selection.
crumbs: 6  Genetic markers and primer design

title: Genetic markers and primer design
text:
Common genetic markers
Different taxonomic groups require different genetic markers because evolutionary rates vary among genes and organisms.

12S rRNA
The mitochondrial 12S ribosomal RNA gene is one of the most widely used markers for vertebrate eDNA studies. Its conserved primer-binding regions and short variable regions make it particularly suitable for degraded environmental DNA.
Typical applications include fish, amphibians, reptiles, birds, and mammals.


Cytochrome c oxidase subunit I (COI)
COI is the standard DNA barcode for animals and provides excellent species-level resolution. However, the full-length barcode is approximately 650 base pairs long, making it less suitable for fragmented environmental DNA. Consequently, many eDNA studies employ shorter COI mini-barcodes.


16S rRNA
The 16S ribosomal RNA gene is widely used to study bacteria and archaea. Conserved regions permit universal amplification, while variable regions enable taxonomic classification.


18S rRNA
The 18S ribosomal RNA gene is highly conserved and is commonly used to investigate diverse eukaryotic communities, particularly protists and other microscopic organisms.
crumbs: 6  Genetic markers and primer design

title: Genetic markers and primer design
text:
Primer design
PCR requires two short DNA molecules known as primers. A forward primer binds to one DNA strand. A reverse primer binds to the complementary strand. Together, they define the DNA region that will be amplified. Primers are typically 18–30 nucleotides long and are designed to bind to conserved regions that flank a variable DNA sequence. Because PCR can amplify only the DNA located between the primers, primer design determines which organisms are detected. Poorly designed primers may fail to amplify certain species, resulting in biased biodiversity estimates.



How primers work
crumbs: 6  Genetic markers and primer design

title: Genetic markers and primer design
text:
Universal and taxon-specific primers
Not all primers are designed for the same purpose. Universal primers amplify DNA from a wide range of organisms and are commonly used in biodiversity surveys. Taxon-specific primers target particular groups, such as fish, amphibians or mammals, while excluding unrelated organisms. The choice between universal and taxon-specific primers depends on the objectives of the study.
crumbs: 6  Genetic markers and primer design

title: Genetic markers and primer design
text:
Marker selection in eDNA studies
Choosing an appropriate marker requires balancing several competing factors. Researchers must consider:

the target taxonomic group
expected DNA degradation
required taxonomic resolution
availability of reference sequences
sequencing platform
study objectives

No marker is universally superior. The most appropriate marker depends on the biological question being addressed.
crumbs: 6  Genetic markers and primer design

title: Genetic markers and primer design
text:
From marker selection to PCR
Marker selection begins by defining the target organisms or taxonomic group to be studied. An appropriate genetic marker is then chosen based on the objectives of the study and the characteristics of the target DNA. PCR primers are designed to bind to conserved regions flanking the marker, allowing the intervening variable region to be amplified. The amplified DNA is subsequently sequenced, and the resulting sequences are compared with reference databases to identify the species present in the environmental sample.
crumbs: 6  Genetic markers and primer design

title: Genetic markers and primer design
text:
Connecting the concepts
Genetic markers are short regions of DNA selected because they contain both conserved and variable sequences. Conserved regions provide reliable binding sites for PCR primers, while the intervening variable region contains the genetic differences required for species identification. Marker selection therefore determines which organisms can be detected and how accurately they can be identified.
crumbs: 6  Genetic markers and primer design

title: Genetic markers and primer design
text:
Key concepts
At the end of this chapter, the most important ideas are:

Genetic markers are short DNA regions used for species identification.
An ideal marker combines conserved primer-binding sites with variable internal sequences.
Different taxonomic groups require different genetic markers.
PCR primers determine which DNA regions are amplified.
Marker choice directly influences detection success and taxonomic resolution.
crumbs: 6  Genetic markers and primer design

title: DNA extraction and Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR)
text:
DNA extraction
Previous chapters introduced the principles of environmental DNA (eDNA), explained why mitochondrial DNA is widely used for species detection, and described how genetic markers and primers are selected. However, selecting the appropriate marker is only the beginning of the analytical process.
Environmental samples typically contain extremely small quantities of degraded DNA mixed with sediments, microorganisms, organic matter and substances that can interfere with molecular analyses. Before species can be identified, DNA must first be isolated from the sample and then amplified to produce sufficient copies for sequencing.
DNA extraction and polymerase chain reaction (PCR) form the foundation of every eDNA workflow. The quality of the extracted DNA and the efficiency of PCR directly influence species detection, making these two laboratory procedures among the most critical steps in environmental DNA analysis.
DNA extraction is the process of isolating DNA from environmental material while removing substances that may interfere with downstream analyses. In eDNA studies, DNA may be extracted from water filters, soil, sediment, biofilms or air filters. Regardless of sample type, the objective remains the same: to recover as much target DNA as possible while minimising contamination and DNA loss. Because environmental DNA is often present in extremely low concentrations, efficient extraction is essential for successful species detection.
crumbs: 7  DNA extraction and Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR)

title: DNA extraction and Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR)
text:
Stages of DNA extraction
Although extraction protocols vary, they generally involve four principal steps.

Cell lysis
The first stage involves breaking open cells and mitochondria to release DNA. This is achieved using combinations of detergents, enzymes and mechanical disruption.


Removal of contaminants
Proteins, lipids and other cellular components are removed using chemical reagents or purification columns.


DNA purification
The released DNA is separated from inhibitory compounds such as humic acids, which are common in soil and sediment samples.


DNA elution
Finally, purified DNA is dissolved in a suitable buffer or sterile water, producing a DNA extract ready for molecular analysis.
crumbs: 7  DNA extraction and Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR)

title: DNA extraction and Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR)
text:
Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR)
Environmental DNA is typically present in quantities far below the detection limits of most analytical methods. PCR overcomes this limitation by producing millions of copies of a selected DNA fragment. Rather than copying an entire genome, PCR amplifies only the region located between two primers. Because primers bind to conserved sequences surrounding a genetic marker, only the target region is copied. PCR therefore converts minute quantities of environmental DNA into sufficient material for sequencing and species identification.
crumbs: 7  DNA extraction and Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR)

title: DNA extraction and Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR)
text:
Components of PCR
A PCR reaction requires several essential components.

Template DNA
The extracted environmental DNA containing the target sequence.


Primers
Forward and reverse primers define the DNA region to be amplified.


DNA polymerase
A heat-stable enzyme, commonly Taq polymerase, synthesises new DNA strands.


Nucleotides (dNTPs)
The building blocks required to synthesise new DNA molecules.


Buffer and magnesium ions
These provide the optimal chemical conditions for DNA polymerase activity.
crumbs: 7  DNA extraction and Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR)

title: DNA extraction and Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR)
text:
The PCR cycle
PCR consists of repeated cycles, each containing three stages.

Denaturation
The reaction is heated to approximately 94–95°C, causing the two DNA strands to separate.



Denaturation




Annealing
The temperature is lowered to allow primers to bind to complementary DNA sequences.



Annealing




Extension
The temperature is increased to approximately 72°C, enabling DNA polymerase to synthesise new DNA strands.
Each cycle approximately doubles the amount of target DNA.
After 30–40 cycles, millions to billions of copies of the original DNA fragment may be produced.
crumbs: 7  DNA extraction and Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR)

title: DNA extraction and Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR)
text:
Exponential amplification
PCR is often described as an exponential amplification process.



Amplification
crumbs: 7  DNA extraction and Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR)

title: DNA extraction and Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR)
text:
PCR inhibition
Environmental samples frequently contain compounds that interfere with PCR. Common inhibitors include:

humic acids
tannins
heavy metals
polysaccharides
phenolic compounds

These substances reduce DNA polymerase activity or prevent primers from binding efficiently, leading to weak amplification or false-negative results. Consequently, removing inhibitors during DNA extraction is a critical component of eDNA analysis.
crumbs: 7  DNA extraction and Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR)

title: DNA extraction and Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR)
text:
Preventing contamination
Because PCR is capable of amplifying even a single DNA molecule, contamination represents one of the greatest challenges in molecular laboratories. Potential contamination sources include:

previous PCR products
laboratory equipment
sampling equipment
airborne DNA
operator handling

To minimise contamination, laboratories employ dedicated work areas, sterile consumables, negative controls and strict quality assurance procedures.
crumbs: 7  DNA extraction and Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR)

title: DNA extraction and Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR)
section: From environmental sample to PCR product
text:
From environmental sample to PCR product
Environmental samples are collected and filtered to concentrate DNA. DNA is then extracted by lysing cells, removing contaminants and purifying the genetic material. The purified DNA serves as the template for PCR, where primers bind to conserved regions surrounding the target marker. Through repeated cycles of denaturation, annealing and extension, DNA polymerase amplifies the target sequence millions of times. The amplified DNA is then ready for sequencing and subsequent species identification.
crumbs: 7  DNA extraction and Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR)

title: DNA extraction and Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR)
text:
Connecting the concepts
DNA extraction isolates genetic material from complex environmental samples, while PCR selectively amplifies short target regions suitable for sequencing. Together, these techniques transform minute quantities of degraded environmental DNA into sufficient genetic material for accurate species identification.
crumbs: 7  DNA extraction and Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR)

title: DNA extraction and Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR)
text:
Key concepts
At the end of this chapter, the most important ideas are:

DNA extraction isolates DNA while removing contaminants and inhibitors.
Environmental DNA is often present in low concentrations and is highly fragmented.
PCR selectively amplifies DNA located between two primers.
PCR consists of denaturation, annealing and extension.
PCR amplification is exponential during the early stages of the reaction.
Contamination control is essential for reliable eDNA analyses.
crumbs: 7  DNA extraction and Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR)

title: DNA sequencing technologies
text:
What is DNA sequencing?
Polymerase chain reaction (PCR) produces millions of copies of a selected DNA fragment, but amplification alone does not reveal the identity of the organisms present in an environmental sample. To identify species, the exact order of nucleotides within each amplified DNA fragment must be determined.
This process, known as DNA sequencing, converts DNA molecules into digital sequence data that can be compared with reference databases. Advances in sequencing technology have transformed environmental DNA research, allowing millions of DNA fragments from hundreds or thousands of species to be analysed simultaneously.
This chapter introduces the principles of DNA sequencing and describes the technologies that have made modern eDNA metabarcoding possible.
DNA sequencing is the process of determining the precise order of nucleotides within a DNA molecule. For example, sequencing may produce the following DNA fragment:
ATGCGTTACCGATG…
The sequence itself is the biological information used for species identification. Once obtained, it can be compared with reference databases to determine the organism from which it originated. Unlike PCR, which simply copies DNA, sequencing reveals its nucleotide composition.

title: DNA sequencing technologies
text:
First-generation sequencing: Sanger sequencing
The first widely adopted DNA sequencing method was developed by Frederick Sanger in 1977. Sanger sequencing determines DNA sequences by incorporating fluorescently labelled chain-terminating nucleotides during DNA synthesis. The resulting fragments are separated by size, allowing the nucleotide sequence to be reconstructed. Sanger sequencing produces highly accurate reads of approximately 700–1,000 base pairs. However, it sequences only one DNA fragment at a time. For this reason, Sanger sequencing is well suited to analysing individual specimens but is generally unsuitable for complex environmental samples containing DNA from many species.

title: DNA sequencing technologies
text:
Second-generation sequencing (Next-generation sequencing)
The development of next-generation sequencing (NGS) transformed molecular ecology by allowing millions of DNA fragments to be sequenced simultaneously. Instead of analysing a single DNA molecule, NGS sequences large numbers of DNA fragments in parallel, dramatically increasing speed while reducing cost per sequence. This capability makes NGS ideally suited for eDNA metabarcoding, where environmental samples may contain DNA from hundreds of species. Among the available platforms, Illumina sequencing has become the most widely used in environmental DNA research because of its high accuracy and throughput.

Illumina sequencing
Illumina sequencing works by attaching amplified DNA fragments to a flow cell, where each fragment is copied to form a small cluster of identical molecules. During sequencing, fluorescently labelled nucleotides are added one base at a time. Each incorporated nucleotide emits a characteristic colour that is detected by a camera. The sequence of colours is converted into the corresponding DNA sequence. Illumina platforms typically produce short sequence reads ranging from approximately 150 to 300 base pairs, making them well suited to the short DNA fragments commonly recovered in environmental samples.

title: DNA sequencing technologies
text:
Third-generation sequencing
Third-generation sequencing technologies analyse individual DNA molecules without requiring extensive amplification. Two widely used platforms are:

Pacific Biosciences (PacBio), which produces highly accurate long reads through circular consensus sequencing.
Oxford Nanopore Technologies, which measures changes in electrical current as DNA molecules pass through microscopic nanopores.

These technologies generate much longer sequence reads than Illumina, often exceeding several thousand base pairs. Although long-read sequencing is becoming increasingly important, most eDNA metabarcoding studies continue to rely on short-read sequencing because environmental DNA is typically highly fragmented.

title: DNA sequencing technologies
text:
Sequencing reads
Each DNA fragment generated during sequencing is known as a read. An environmental sample may produce thousands to millions of reads, each representing a short DNA sequence derived from one of the many organisms present in the sample.
The total number of reads obtained for a sample is referred to as its sequencing depth. Sequencing depth is an important measure because it influences the likelihood of detecting species, particularly those that are rare or present in low abundance. In general, a greater sequencing depth increases the probability of recovering DNA from uncommon species, although it also increases sequencing costs and may eventually yield diminishing returns.
Before species identification, sequencing reads undergo quality filtering to remove low-quality sequences, sequencing errors and artefacts that could compromise downstream analyses. The remaining high-quality reads are then used for bioinformatic processing, where they are compared with reference databases to identify the species present in the environmental sample.

title: DNA sequencing technologies
text:
Choosing a sequencing platform
The choice of sequencing platform depends on several factors, including:

the objectives of the study
expected DNA fragment length
sequencing accuracy
cost
throughput
available bioinformatic resources

For most eDNA metabarcoding applications, Illumina platforms provide an effective balance between read quality, throughput and cost. Long-read technologies are becoming increasingly valuable for genome assembly, population genomics and certain specialised eDNA applications.

title: DNA sequencing technologies
section: From PCR product to DNA sequence
text:
From PCR product to DNA sequence
Following PCR amplification, DNA fragments are prepared for sequencing by attaching platform-specific adapters and sample-identifying indices. The sequencing instrument then determines the order of nucleotides within each amplified fragment, producing millions of individual sequence reads. These digital sequences are exported for computational analysis, where they are filtered, compared with reference databases and assigned to species.

title: DNA sequencing technologies
text:
Connecting the concepts
PCR generates millions of copies of a selected DNA marker, while sequencing determines the exact nucleotide sequence of each amplified fragment. These sequences provide the genetic information needed for bioinformatic analysis and species identification.

title: DNA sequencing technologies
text:
Key concepts
At the end of this chapter, the most important ideas are:

DNA sequencing determines the order of nucleotides in a DNA molecule.
PCR amplifies DNA, whereas sequencing reveals its nucleotide sequence.
Sanger sequencing analyses one DNA fragment at a time.
Next-generation sequencing enables millions of DNA fragments to be sequenced simultaneously.
Illumina sequencing is the most widely used platform for eDNA metabarcoding.
Sequencing reads provide the raw data for bioinformatic analysis.

title: Bioinformatics and taxonomic assignment
text:
What is bioinformatics?
DNA sequencing generates millions of short DNA sequences, known as reads, from every environmental sample. However, these raw sequencing data do not immediately reveal which species are present. Before ecological interpretation is possible, the reads must undergo a series of computational analyses to remove sequencing errors, eliminate artefacts and assign each sequence to its most likely taxonomic origin.
This process is known as bioinformatics. Bioinformatic analysis transforms raw sequencing data into species lists, estimates of biodiversity and community composition, making it an essential component of every environmental DNA (eDNA) study.
Bioinformatics is the application of computational methods to store, process and analyse biological data.
In eDNA studies, bioinformatics converts millions of raw DNA sequences into meaningful biological information. The objective is to determine which organisms contributed DNA to the environmental sample while minimising errors introduced during PCR and sequencing.
Without bioinformatics, sequencing data would simply consist of millions of strings of nucleotide letters with no biological interpretation.

title: Bioinformatics and taxonomic assignment
text:
The bioinformatic workflow
Although individual pipelines differ, most eDNA analyses follow a similar sequence of steps. First, sequencing reads are imported into specialised software. Low-quality reads and sequencing artefacts are removed. Primer and adapter sequences are trimmed. Identical or highly similar sequences are grouped. Representative sequences are compared with reference databases. Finally, taxonomic identities are assigned and summarised for ecological analysis. Each stage improves the quality and reliability of the final dataset.

title: Bioinformatics and taxonomic assignment
text:
Quality filtering
Sequencing instruments occasionally produce errors, including incorrect nucleotides, incomplete reads or low-confidence base calls. Quality filtering removes these unreliable sequences before further analysis.
Typical filtering procedures include:

removing short reads
excluding low-quality sequences
trimming poor-quality bases
discarding reads containing ambiguous nucleotides (N)

Quality filtering reduces false species detections and improves confidence in downstream analyses.

title: Bioinformatics and taxonomic assignment
text:
Removing chimeras
PCR occasionally produces artificial DNA sequences known as chimeras.
A chimera is formed when fragments from two different DNA molecules are mistakenly joined together during amplification.
Because chimeras do not represent real biological sequences, they must be detected and removed before taxonomic assignment.
Modern bioinformatic software identifies chimeras by comparing sequences with more abundant parental sequences.

title: Bioinformatics and taxonomic assignment
text:
OTUs and ASVs
Once poor-quality sequences have been removed, similar sequences are grouped to simplify analysis. Historically, sequences sharing a predefined level of similarity (commonly 97%) were clustered into Operational Taxonomic Units (OTUs). More recently, many studies have adopted Amplicon Sequence Variants (ASVs). Unlike OTUs, ASVs distinguish sequences differing by as little as a single nucleotide, providing higher taxonomic resolution and greater reproducibility between studies. Today, ASVs are increasingly regarded as the preferred approach for analysing eDNA metabarcoding data.

title: Bioinformatics and taxonomic assignment
text:
Taxonomic assignment
After quality filtering, each sequence is compared with reference databases containing DNA sequences from known organisms. The closest matching reference sequence is used to assign a taxonomic identity. Depending on sequence quality and database completeness, identification may be possible at different taxonomic levels, including species, genus, family, and order. Not every sequence can be assigned to species level. Some sequences may represent organisms absent from current reference databases.

title: Bioinformatics and taxonomic assignment
text:
Reference databases
Accurate species identification depends on comprehensive and reliable reference databases.
Several databases are commonly used in eDNA studies.

GenBank
GenBank is the world’s largest public nucleotide sequence database and contains DNA sequences from a wide variety of organisms.


BOLD (Barcode of Life Data System)
BOLD specialises in DNA barcode sequences and is widely used for animal identification, particularly using the COI gene.

title: Bioinformatics and taxonomic assignment
text:
Sources of error
Even after careful laboratory procedures, bioinformatic analyses remain susceptible to error.
Potential sources include:

sequencing errors
PCR artefacts
contamination
incomplete reference databases
incorrect database annotations
closely related species sharing similar DNA sequences

For these reasons, bioinformatic results should always be interpreted alongside appropriate laboratory controls and ecological knowledge.

title: Bioinformatics and taxonomic assignment
section: From raw reads to species list
text:
From raw reads to species list
The bioinformatic workflow can be summarised as follows.
Sequencing generates millions of DNA reads. Low-quality reads, sequencing errors and chimeras are removed through quality filtering. The remaining high-quality sequences are grouped into ASVs or OTUs and compared with reference databases. Based on the closest matches, taxonomic identities are assigned, producing a list of species detected in the environmental sample.

title: Bioinformatics and taxonomic assignment
text:
Connecting the concepts
DNA sequencing produces millions of short reads, but these reads have little biological meaning until they are processed computationally. Bioinformatics filters sequencing errors, removes artefacts and compares DNA sequences with reference databases, transforming raw sequence data into reliable species identifications.

title: Bioinformatics and taxonomic assignment
text:
Key concepts
At the end of this chapter, the most important ideas are:

Bioinformatics converts raw sequencing reads into biological information.
Quality filtering removes unreliable DNA sequences.
Chimeras are artificial sequences produced during PCR.
ASVs provide higher sequence resolution than traditional OTUs.
Species identification depends on comparison with reference databases.
Database quality strongly influences taxonomic accuracy.

title: eDNA timeline overview
text: 1980s: Foundations of DNA fingerprinting and environmental DNA

title: eDNA timeline overview
text:
DNA fingerprinting established (Jeffreys et al. 1985)

Discovery of hypervariable minisatellite regions.
First individual-specific DNA “barcode.”
Laid the foundation for forensic genetics, paternity testing, and later molecular ecology.




The term “environmental DNA” introduced (Ogram et al. 1987)

Developed methods to extract intracellular and extracellular DNA directly from sediments.
Introduced the concept of recovering DNA directly from environmental samples without isolating organisms.

PCR enables DNA fingerprinting from tiny samples

Jeffreys et al. (1988): PCR amplification of minisatellites made DNA fingerprinting practical from very small samples.
Hoelzel et al. (1988): DNA fingerprinting applied to whale skin biopsies, demonstrating non-lethal genetic monitoring of wildlife.


Major breakthrough of the decade: DNA became a practical tool for identifying individuals, and environmental DNA was first recognised as a recoverable genetic resource.

title: eDNA timeline overview
section: 1990s: From DNA fingerprinting to DNA barcodes and early environmental DNA applications
text:
1990s: From DNA fingerprinting to DNA barcodes and early environmental DNA applications


Giovannoni et al. 1990 analysed 16S ribosomal RNA (rRNA) gene sequences directly from a microbial community in the Sargasso Sea, providing one of the first culture-independent surveys of environmental microbial diversity.
Amos et al. 1992 demonstrated that DNA recovered from naturally shed material, such as whale skin, could be used for genetic analysis.
Arnot et al. 1993 Introduced the concept of using distinctive genetic sequence patterns as biological identifiers, and the term “DNA barcode” was first used to describe molecular signatures capable of distinguishing biological samples.
Paget et al. 1998 demonstrated that plant DNA could be recovered directly from soil, providing early evidence that environmental samples preserve genetic traces of larger organisms.


Major breakthrough of the decade: DNA analysis expanded from identifying individual organisms to characterising entire biological communities. Environmental sequencing revealed hidden microbial diversity, DNA barcoding introduced genetic identification frameworks, and early macrobial eDNA studies demonstrated that environmental samples could act as archives of biodiversity. Together, these advances established the foundations of modern eDNA metabarcoding, biodiversity assessment, and molecular ecology.

title: eDNA timeline overview
section: 2000s: Environmental DNA becomes a tool for biodiversity reconstruction, pollution tracking, and species detection
text:
2000s: Environmental DNA becomes a tool for biodiversity reconstruction, pollution tracking, and species detection

DNA persistence and the ecological role of dissolved DNA (Matsui et al. 2001)

Demonstrated that extracellular dissolved DNA (dDNA) can persist in aquatic environments, with DNA fragments of approximately 400 bp surviving for up to 7 days at 18°C.
Provided early experimental evidence that environmental DNA is not immediately degraded after release into water, supporting its use as a molecular signal for species detection.
Highlighted the natural occurrence of dissolved extracellular DNA, which enters ecosystems through cell death, shedding of tissues, and biological secretions.
Showed that environmental DNA persistence is influenced by environmental factors including UV radiation, temperature, and nuclease activity.
Established that dissolved DNA is not only genetic information but also an important ecological resource, providing microorganisms with carbon, nitrogen, and phosphorus.
Demonstrated that microbes can utilise extracellular DNA through enzymatic degradation, recycling genetic material as nutrients.

UV radiation identified as a major factor affecting DNA persistence (Ravanet et al. 2001)

Highlighted UV exposure as an important abiotic factor influencing the persistence and detectability of environmental DNA.
Contributed to understanding why eDNA signals vary between habitats and why DNA preservation differs among ecosystems.

First metagenetic analysis of soil eDNA (Willerslev et al. 2003)

Demonstrated that DNA extracted from permafrost and sediment samples could reveal past biodiversity in the absence of visible fossils or macrofossils.
Recovered ancient DNA sequences from Siberian permafrost cores dating from 400,000 to 10,000 years ago, including plant taxa and megafaunal species such as mammoth, bison, and horse.
Showed that sedimentary DNA could reconstruct historical changes in ecosystem composition and biodiversity through time.
Established environmental DNA as a powerful archive for studying past ecosystems and long-term ecological change.

Environmental DNA as a resource for microorganisms (Chen and Dubnau 2004)

Expanded understanding of the ecological functions of extracellular DNA by showing that bacteria can actively acquire environmental DNA.
Demonstrated two major roles of environmental DNA for microorganisms:

Genetic resource: DNA from related bacteria may provide templates for repairing damaged genetic material.
Nutritional resource: DNA can be degraded and used as a source of carbon, nitrogen, and phosphorus.

Highlighted that extracellular DNA is an active component of microbial ecosystems rather than simply degraded biological material.

First microbial and faecal eDNA detection in river water (Martellini et al. 2005)

Developed a PCR-based method to detect and distinguish faecal contamination from human, bovine, ovine, and porcine sources in aquatic environments.
Used species-specific mitochondrial DNA markers to identify the origin of pollution in wastewater, agricultural runoff, and river samples.
Represented the first application of eukaryotic genetic material from water samples to trace animal-derived pollution sources.
Demonstrated the potential of eDNA approaches for environmental monitoring, water quality assessment, and microbial source tracking.

eDNA enables detection of invasive and cryptic species (Ficetola et al. 2008)

Developed one of the first widely recognised applications of eDNA for detecting a target species in freshwater ecosystems.
Used species-specific mitochondrial DNA primers to detect American bullfrog DNA in controlled experiments and natural wetlands.
Successfully identified populations even when species densities were low and traditional surveys were ineffective.
Demonstrated that eDNA could provide a sensitive, non-invasive method for monitoring invasive, rare, and difficult-to-detect species.
Highlighted challenges associated with interpreting eDNA signals, including uncertainty over how long DNA remains detectable after an organism leaves an environment.

DNA barcoding becomes integrated into ecology (Valentini et al. 2008)

Provided an overview of DNA barcoding as a method for species identification using standardised genetic markers.
Demonstrated the value of molecular identification tools for ecological studies, biodiversity surveys, and species monitoring.
Helped connect traditional ecological research with emerging molecular approaches.

Non-invasive genetics advances ecological monitoring (Beja-Pereira et al. 2009)

Reviewed the development of non-invasive genetic sampling approaches for wildlife studies.
Showed how DNA obtained from naturally shed biological material could transform population monitoring and conservation genetics.
Highlighted technological improvements that increased the feasibility of studying wildlife without capture or disturbance.
Provided important methodological foundations for later vertebrate eDNA applications.


Major breakthrough of the decade: Environmental DNA transitioned from a concept describing DNA persistence in the environment into a practical ecological monitoring tool. During the 2000s, researchers demonstrated that DNA could be recovered from sediments, water, and naturally shed material to reconstruct past ecosystems, trace pollution sources, and detect rare or invasive species. Advances in PCR, DNA barcoding, and non-invasive genetics transformed eDNA from a theoretical possibility into a powerful method for biodiversity assessment and conservation biology.
